hiv-aids-primary.name: Patient
hiv-aids-primary.chiefComplaint: Man with unintentional weight loss, oral thrush, and chronic diarrhea
hiv-aids-primary.variantDescription: Screening, CD4 count staging, opportunistic infections, ART principles, PrEP/PEP
keyFindings: Screen: 4th gen Ag/Ab combo test (p24 antigen + HIV-1/2 antibodies)
CD4 <200: AIDS definition; opportunistic infection prophylaxis thresholds
CD4 <200: PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX); <100: Toxoplasma prophylaxis; <50: MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin)
ART for ALL patients regardless of CD4 count (treat early)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of HIV/AIDS

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Screen: 4th gen Ag/Ab combo test (p24 antigen + HIV-1/2 antibodies)
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

questions.stem: A patient with HIV has a CD4 count of 75 cells/μL and presents with subacute headache, fever, and confusion. India ink stain of CSF is positive. What is the recommended initial treatment?

options.text: IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole
options.explanation: Positive India ink indicates Cryptococcus neoformans meningitis. Per IDSA/WHO guidelines, induction therapy is amphotericin B + flucytosine × 2 weeks, followed by consolidation with fluconazole 400mg/day × 8 weeks, then maintenance fluconazole 200mg/day. Do NOT start ART immediately — wait 4-6 weeks to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS).
Fluconazole monotherapy for induction has significantly higher mortality than amphotericin-based regimens. It is used for consolidation and maintenance, not induction.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin for bacterial meningitis coverage
options.explanation: The India ink positivity confirms cryptococcal meningitis. Bacterial meningitis coverage is not indicated when the organism is identified. Antifungal therapy is required.

options.text: Start ART immediately along with amphotericin B
options.explanation: Starting ART simultaneously with antifungal induction significantly increases risk of IRIS, which has high mortality in cryptococcal meningitis. ART should be deferred 4-6 weeks per guidelines.

tuberculosis-primary.name: Patient
tuberculosis-primary.chiefComplaint: Immigrant with chronic productive cough, night sweats, and weight loss
tuberculosis-primary.variantDescription: Latent vs active TB, TST vs IGRA, treatment regimens, MDR-TB approach
keyFindings: Latent TB: positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious, normal CXR
Active TB: symptoms (cough >3 weeks, hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss) + abnormal CXR (upper lobe cavitation)
Diagnosis: sputum AFB smear and culture, nucleic acid amplification test (GeneXpert)
Active TB treatment: RIPE (Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) × 2 months → RI × 4 months
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Tuberculosis

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Latent TB: positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious, normal CXR
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.
